title: LGBTQ family building: A guide for prospective parents. By Abbie E.Goldberg, Washington, DC: American Psychological Association. 2022. pp. 295. $16.99 (paperback). ISBN: 978‐1‐4338‐3392‐2
abstract: LGBTQ young people are increasingly considering parenthood for themselves (Patterson & Riskind, 2010; Reczek, 2020; Riskind et al., 2013; Simon et al., 2018), yet most books on family building assume a heterosexual, cisgender audience. Rarely do mainstream texts address the unique decisions and experiences of LGBTQ people navigating prospective parenthood in a heteronormative society. Abbie Goldberg's LGBTQ Family Building: A Guide for Prospective Parents fills this void in the parenting literature. Evident by the book's title, Goldberg's audience is LGBTQ prospective parents, and her text is a master class for academics in how to write accessibly for a lay audience. Distilling actionable recommendations from empirical research, she walks her readers through the practical and emotional journey of prospective LGBTQ parenthood, leaving no stone unturned in the questions she invites readers to engage with along the way. “Becoming a parent is a major life decision,” Goldberg writes, “and this book is designed to help you engage with that decision: the ‘if’, the ‘how’, the ‘when’, and all that comes after” (Goldberg, 2022, p. 5). freedom to define their own roles as parents, a tendency to share parenting responsibilities more equally than cisgender (cis) heterosexual folks, a deep appreciation for (i.e., not “taking for granted”) the possibility of parenthood, and personal experiences with stigma and discrimination that may foster resilience and empathy (e.g., toward their children)… (p. 4) With each chapter, Goldberg moves through key steps and decision points in the journey to becoming a parent. Chapter 1 covers the process of deciding to become a parent for LGBTQ people, addressing potential obstacles such as internalized stigmas, discrimination, and partner disinterest in parenthood. Chapters 2 through 4 guide readers through the process of selecting a pathway to parenthood, including adoption (Chapter 2), donor insemination (Chapter 3), and surrogacy (Chapter 4). Chapter 5 addresses how to assess the LGBTQ friendliness and overall “fit” of adoption agencies, health care providers, childcare centers, and other institutions, complete with questions and checklists to assist the evaluative process. Chapter 6 addresses the transition to parenthood, preparing readers for potential changes in identity, roles, and relationships, including a discussion of same-gender couples' division of labor. Chapter 7 addresses parenting in the early years, including preparing for heteronormativity (e.g., strategies for minimizing and managing heterosexist bias at children's schools) as well as universal early parenting challenges. Each chapter opens with a vignette of LGBTQ parents or prospective parents who are grappling with a complex issue or decision along their journey to parenthood, and each chapter concludes with a compilation of additional resources. This book is meant to be highly practical. Unlike the trope of the self-help book heavy in fluff and lacking in substance, Goldberg's accessible guide offers an impressive collection of keen, useful, thought-provoking considerations for LGBTQ prospective parents. Rather than presenting actionable recommendations as universally effective, she carefully couches them within contextual considerations so that readers can assess their utility for themselves. Given the nature of this text (i.e., a practical guide written for a lay audience), theory is not explicitly used as an organizing or interpretative framework. You will not find headings for intersectionality or minority stress theory within or across chapters. However, theory is undeniably there, guiding Goldberg's approach to mapping the options and decision points for LGBTQ prospective parents along their journey toward parenthood. In this analysis, we bring theory to the fore. We illustrate how Goldberg's text models how to translate theoretical principles into clear, relatable examples that readers can easily comprehend—in other words, how to “show” theory without burdening readers with unnecessary jargon. (In fact, instructors of theories courses looking for a text that their students can examine through a theoretical lens would find many opportunities in this book.) First, we examine the book through the lens of intersectionality, assessing the ways in which the author invites readers to consider LGBTQ parenting in relationship with other identities and experiences which impact pathways to parenthood. Second, we consider the book's place in a resilience-focused family science, situating the text in the current sociopolitical moment of both progress toward and attacks on LGBTQ rights. We conclude with insights gleaned from Goldberg's text about underdeveloped areas of research on LGBTQ parenting. Though implicit, Goldberg unmistakably applies one overarching framework in particular: intersectionality. Intersectionality deconstructs any one social identity (e.g., queer, parent) as a monolithic category and exposes the ways in which our experiences are shaped by the complex, interwoven nature of our social identities (Allen & Henderson, 2022; Crenshaw, 1991; Few-Demo & Allen, 2020). More specifically, intersectionality draws attention to the ways in which identities that are tied to forms of stratification (e.g., race, gender, social class, sexual identity, romantic orientations, nationality, and ability status) intersect to act as a matrix of domination or oppression (Collins, 1990), bestowing “relative amounts of oppression and privilege that challenge or benefit individual and family life” (Allen & Henderson, 2022, p. 373). Society tends to privilege biogenetic connections. In turn, you should consider with your partner various dimensions of power and privilege (e.g., race, ability) as you evaluate what it would be like for each of you to carry the child, be genetically related to the child, and be more easily recognizable as the child's parent. For example, if you are a White, able-bodied person who is also the gestational parent, and your partner is a Latinx, (dis)abled person who is the nongestational parent, how might this intensify existing differences in privilege and social capital, both within your relationship and in terms of how you are viewed by outsiders? (p. 82) Notice how Goldberg applies an intersectional framework while keeping the material practical and accessible to readers. She offers concrete examples of how intersecting identities—identities that are tied to systems of domination or oppression—will impact how readers move through the world as LGBTQ parents and thus should not be ignored when making decisions about parenthood. She encourages readers to take seriously how decisions about parenthood status (e.g., who will be the genetic and/or gestational parent) might intensify pre-existing inequities in power and privilege, inviting readers to imagine possible scenarios to guide these decisions (e.g., how do partners anticipate feeling about or reacting to social invisibility or differential treatment?). Goldberg moves beyond mere description of social experiences toward critical examination of “the fluidity, variability, and temporality of interactive processes that occur between and within multiple social groups, institutions, and social practices” (Few-Demo, 2014, p. 170). In other words, she connects individual experience to broader social contexts, encouraging readers to consider how their choices are embedded within systems of social stratification that will impact their choices and experiences. Critically, in situating parenthood decisions within the realities of systemic oppression, Goldberg maintains an emphasis on human agency. Consistent with an intersectional feminist approach, she does not suggest that LGBTQ prospective parents are passively acted upon by systems of oppression or privilege, but rather are actively negotiating parenting decisions in the context of both constraining and enabling forces (Allen, 2023; Few-Demo, 2014). Maintaining an emphasis on readers' agency, Goldberg offers strategies for managing inequities tied to LGBTQ parents' intersectional identities. For example, to counteract the potential for social invisibility of a non-gestational parent, she offers strategies employed by LGBTQ parents such as “giving the child the nongestational parent's last name; having the child's sperm donor resemble the nongestational parent to the extent possible; and having the nongestational parent take on an active, involved role in relation to the child” (p. 82–83). You very well may not be able to find an [adoption] agency or provider that checks all of your boxes… Some providers may not be well informed and experienced with LGBTQ clients but are happy to receive and incorporate feedback. In other cases, however, you may be making trade-offs—for example, you may choose an adoption agency that is well-matched to your morals and values but has limited experience with LGBTQ folks over an agency that has extensive experience with LGBTQ adopters but is grounded in practices or ideals that you find questionable or problematic. (p. 154) The realism of this message acknowledges the restriction of choice based on external constraints (e.g., due to location and financial resources) but does not disempower prospective parents by diminishing their agency. Another way in which Goldberg applies an intersectional perspective is through her careful attention to the sometimes-complex histories of the pathways to parenthood she describes, including adoption and surrogacy. Indeed, intersectional analyses require a commitment to situating issues within sociohistorical contexts (Few-Demo, 2014), and perhaps nowhere is this more important than in resources for lay audiences, where information is often presented as ahistorical. For example, in Chapter 4, Goldberg introduces surrogacy—a high-cost pathway to parenthood—and grounds the discussion in context. She informs readers of the overrepresentation of wealthy, gay White men who have had the financial capital to pursue surrogacy over other groups. She also addresses transnational surrogacy—sometimes referred to as fertility tourism or reproductive outsourcing which, as Goldberg says, “hints at some of the moral critiques of this particular approach to family building” (p. 111). Goldberg acknowledges that GBQ men who pursue family building via transnational surrogacy “have been criticized for participating in and upholding racialized and heteronormative systems of kinship by virtue of (men) paying for (women) surrogates in less privileged nations…” (p. 112). In educating readers of this context, Goldberg does not condemn or dismiss surrogacy as an option altogether—instead, she maintains an emphasis on informed agency. She provides alternative perspectives on this pathway to parenthood as an opportunity to “rework” kinship networks and encourages readers who are interested in surrogacy to grapple with the “moral and ethical critiques that surround it and potentially seek consultation to facilitate a clear-eyed decision-making process” (p. 112). In doing so, she tells readers what they need to know practically to make their own decisions while acknowledging historical background as an important piece of those decision-making processes. Agency is still a priority, regardless of the ethical gray-area. Similarly, Goldberg addresses the problematic history of transracial adoption, including how the overrepresentation of children of color in the child welfare systems reflects a history of over-policing Black and Brown communities, removing children from their families, and permanently placing them with White families (Lash, 2017; Roberts, 2002). Such practices rest on assumptions of White cultural superiority, or the idea that White families can provide a healthier environment for children of color than their communities of origin. Goldberg emphasizes the “clear need for states to prioritize family preservation” by investing in services that support family stability (p. 49). Here too, Goldberg provides readers with critical historical context while also giving readers the information they need to ethically pursue this pathway to parenthood, should they choose to. For example, she acknowledges criticisms of transracial adoption that White parents cannot adequately prepare children of color for the experience of encountering and navigating racism (Goldberg et al., 2022; Killian & Khanna, 2019). At the same time, Goldberg acknowledges that when parents take seriously the responsibility of fostering their children's racial identity, connect them to same-race contexts, and educate themselves about their children's identities/heritage, they are better positioned to help children develop positive racial identities. Goldberg emphasizes that a more ethical approach to this parenting pathway means committing to these critical responsibilities. It is especially crucial to examine internalized stigmas as they relate to parenthood. LGBTQ people may internalize the notion that LGBTQ people are not good parents or don't deserve to take on that role, and they will inevitably raise children who themselves will be distressed or damaged by having LGBTQ parents….It is important for LGBTQ people to acknowledge these worries or beliefs; recognize where they come from, and seek out information, resources, and research that counter them. (p. 26) Reflexivity is a foundational component of each subsequent chapter. Throughout the text, Goldberg offers an array of reflexive self-assessment tools to “help aid introspection related to family-building desires, motivations, and options” (p. 5) and urges readers to “honestly and deeply interrogate” their beliefs, feelings, and preferences (p. 63). For example, in Chapter 2, Goldberg asks hard but important questions about adoption preferences—for instance, how comfortable and open prospective parents are to adopting a child with prenatal substance exposure (opioids, heroin, alcohol), mental and physical health needs, and (dis)abilities. Goldberg also calls attention to harmful logic when indicating adoption preferences, particularly with respect to race and ethnicity—for instance, White prospective parents indicating openness to adopting a biracial (Black/White) child but not a monoracial Black child with the logic that they can “relate to the White part” and that racial socialization is less necessary (p. 68). (Goldberg notes that many adoption agencies no longer allow this, and that prospective parents who are open to adopting a multiracial child must also be open to adopting a monoracial child of color.) In encouraging readers to engage in deep reflexive work, Goldberg provides a list of activities that folks can engage in to support that introspection, such as reading memoirs and authoritative sources about transracial adoption, attending workshops and trainings, and considering the diversity and acceptance of one's own community. Similarly, Chapter 3 invites readers to engage introspectively with complex decisions surrounding egg donors (e.g., whether to pursue a known donor or unknown donor). Goldberg invites readers to consider questions such as: “How important is it that my child is related to someone I know personally?”, “How important is it that my child has an ongoing relationship with their donor?”, and “How much money am I able or willing to spend on sperm?” (p. 88). These questions support thinking not only around parents' individual values and comfort, but also encourage them to consider their children's positionality. In providing supportive, tangible suggestions for engaging with reflexivity, Goldberg makes the process of learning to think reflexively accessible to readers. More broadly, Goldberg's book offers an opportunity to reflect on its sociohistorical situatedness. We've arrived at a unique point in time for LGBTQ individuals and families in the United States.
